text: The surgeon demonstrates the placement of standard laparoscopic ports used for the procedure, showing their positions on the abdominal surface.
The surgeon initiates the dissection, clearly exposing the left and right middle colic arteries within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the left and right middle colic arteries, applying clips to each vessel in turn to secure them.
The surgeon uses an energy device to divide the vessels, gaining access to the lesser sac. Gentle dissection reveals the posterior aspect of the stomach, and the mesocolon is divided.
The surgeon reaches the right middle colic vessels, carefully dissecting them to clear surrounding tissue before proceeding with the division of these vessels.
The surgeon separates the greater omentum from the colon, revealing the underlying mesocolon.
The surgeon identifies and prepares to divide the gastrocolic ligament, a second sheet often encountered after completing the initial dissection.
The surgeon displays the completed dissection from below, with visible clips applied to the dissected vessels.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels using a medial to lateral dissection approach. The vessels are dissected free, and clips are applied before dividing the vessel.
The surgeon performs a dissection over the top of the duodenum, clearly visualizing the structure below as they reach the point where the duodenum curves from the first part to the second part.
The surgeon performs a lateral to medial exposure of the right hemicolon, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon performs dissection to connect with the previously completed medial to lateral dissection, ensuring continuity in the surgical approach.
The surgeon suspends the window created in the ileocolic region and the window from the division of the middle colic vessels, revealing the right colic artery in the surgical field.
The surgeon frees the descending colon and sigmoid from surrounding tissues using a lateral to medial approach, following the division of the right hemicolon and transverse mesocolon.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon up to the level of the splenic flexure, completing the splenic flexure mobilization by liberating the greater omentum from the colon.

The surgical field shows the stomach at the top of the view, with a clear visualization of the pancreas below. The left hemicolon and mesocolon are visible, where the surgeon identifies individual vessels and divides them using an energy device.
The surgeon places endoclips approximately five centimeters before the rectosigmoid junction to mark the desired location for anastomosis, ensuring no redundant loop of sigmoid remains in the pelvis.
The surgeon clips the segmental sigmoid arteries and divides them using an energy device, focusing on the vascular structures within the sigmoid colon region.
The surgeon returns to the right side of the dissection, where the distal ileum is being transected to facilitate the extraction of the colon.
The surgeon performs a mini Pfannenstiel incision and inserts a wound protector, allowing the colon to be delivered through the incision.
The surgeon exteriorizes the ileum and uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to place interrupted sutures in a side-to-side configuration, stabilizing the anastomosis.
The surgeon creates enterotomies in both the ileum and colon, inserting a linear stapling device to facilitate the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses forceps to prevent knots from catching in the staple line and ensures the anti-mesenteric borders are properly aligned. A transverse stapler is applied across the ileum and colon, completing the side-to-side anastomosis configuration.
The surgeon identifies and transfixed any bleeding points using a 3-0 Vicryl suture, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon buries the staple line using three-zero Vicryl sutures and places a crotch stitch to stabilize the anastomosis.

During re-inspection, the staple line is visible along with the terminal ileum and its loops. The surgeon lifts the small bowel mesentery, tracing it in a straight, non-twisted line back to the duodenum.
